stem: You are the general medical registrar admitting a 65-year-old man with alcohol use disorder who has not eaten for five days, drinking only fortified wine. He has lost 15 percent of his body weight over the past three months. He looks cachectic with temporal wasting. He is alert and orientated and his observations are stable. The nutrition team has asked you to write the initial refeeding plan and electrolyte replacement orders before any oral or enteral nutrition is commenced on the ward today.

data_table.Examination: Cachectic with temporal wasting; soft abdomen; no peripheral oedema; alert and orientated
data_table.Investigations: Potassium 3.2, magnesium 0.65, phosphate 0.75, sodium 138, glucose 4.2, albumin 28, INR 1.2
data_table.Medications: Nil regular on admission; pabrinex prescribed pending decision on timing

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial refeeding plan?

options.text: Give intravenous thiamine, correct potassium, magnesium and phosphate, then commence nutrition at 10 kcal/kg/day with daily electrolyte monitoring and slow titration
options.rationale: High refeeding risk requires thiamine before carbohydrates, proactive electrolyte replacement, slow 10 kcal/kg/day caloric initiation and daily monitoring to prevent cardiac and neurological complications. (Source: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence: Nutrition support in adults)
source_refs: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence: Nutrition support in adults

options.text: Commence a standard 1500 kilocalorie oral diet immediately and replace electrolytes only if levels fall below the laboratory reference range during ward observation
options.rationale: Standard caloric load triggers refeeding syndrome in high-risk patients; calories must start at 10 kcal/kg/day with proactive electrolyte and thiamine replacement first. (Source: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence: Nutrition support in adults)
source_refs: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence: Nutrition support in adults

options.text: Use 10 percent dextrose intravenously as the sole nutritional source for the first 72 hours and defer enteral feeding until biochemistry has fully normalised after admission
options.rationale: High-carbohydrate intravenous dextrose without thiamine precipitates Wernicke encephalopathy and refeeding hypophosphataemia; enteral or oral is preferred with parallel electrolyte support. (Source: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence: Nutrition support in adults)
source_refs: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence: Nutrition support in adults

options.text: Insert a nasogastric tube and run full-strength polymeric feed at 80 mL per hour from day one to meet his estimated full caloric requirement immediately on the ward
options.rationale: Full-rate feeding in high refeeding risk overwhelms intracellular shifts and precipitates cardiac arrhythmia, respiratory failure and seizures; rate must start at 10 kcal/kg/day. (Source: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence: Nutrition support in adults)
source_refs: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence: Nutrition support in adults

options.text: Defer all nutrition for a further five days while focusing solely on alcohol withdrawal management and hydration before any oral or enteral feeding plan is restarted
options.rationale: Extending starvation worsens nutritional deficits without addressing refeeding risk; the correct approach is cautious early nutrition with electrolyte and thiamine support. (Source: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence: Nutrition support in adults)
source_refs: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence: Nutrition support in adults

explanation.summary: Refeeding syndrome occurs when carbohydrate reintroduction in chronically starved patients triggers intracellular shifts of potassium, magnesium and phosphate, precipitating cardiac arrhythmia, respiratory failure, seizures and Wernicke encephalopathy. High-risk criteria include BMI under 16, weight loss over 15 percent in three months, little nutrition for over 10 days, low potassium, phosphate or magnesium pre-feeding. Management is parenteral thiamine 200 to 300 mg before any glucose, proactive electrolyte replacement, slow caloric initiation at 5 to 10 kcal/kg/day with stepwise increase over 4 to 7 days, and daily biochemistry monitoring.
key_points: Thiamine 200 to 300 mg IV before carbohydrate refeeding
Start nutrition at 10 kcal/kg/day in high-risk patients
Replace potassium, magnesium and phosphate proactively
Daily electrolytes until stable for at least one week
explanation.pearls: Pabrinex (B and C vitamins) intravenous two pairs three times daily for 5 days is the Australian first-line for suspected Wernicke encephalopathy and prophylaxis prior to refeeding in chronic alcohol misuse.

sources.label: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence: Nutrition support in adults

stem: You are the endocrinology registrar reviewing a 45-year-old woman with a six-month history of progressive central weight gain, easy bruising on the forearms, proximal muscle weakness on standing from a chair, and new-onset hypertension requiring two antihypertensive medications. She has facial plethora and purple abdominal striae 1.5 cm wide. She has no exogenous corticosteroid use confirmed on pharmacy records. She takes no medications likely to affect cortisol metabolism. She is keen to know what tests will confirm or exclude hypercortisolism today.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 158/96 on two agents, HR 80, weight 92 kg, BMI 33
data_table.Examination: Moon facies; supraclavicular fat pad; purple striae over abdomen and thighs; proximal weakness in hip flexion grade 4/5
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 7.2 percent, fasting glucose 8.1; FBC and UEC unremarkable; ACTH and a.m. cortisol not yet performed
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 5 mg daily; amlodipine 5 mg daily; nil corticosteroids confirmed by pharmacy record

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line screening test?

options.text: Random midday serum cortisol drawn during the next clinic visit as the initial screening test before considering further dynamic investigations of the axis
options.rationale: Random cortisol reflects pulsatile secretion and does not detect loss of diurnal rhythm or cortisol excess; it is not a recommended screening test for Cushing syndrome. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Diagnosis of Cushing syndrome)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Diagnosis of Cushing syndrome

options.text: Serum adrenocorticotrophic hormone level at 09:00 as the single first-line test that distinguishes ACTH-dependent and ACTH-independent forms of Cushing syndrome
options.rationale: ACTH measurement differentiates ACTH-dependent from independent causes but only after hypercortisolism is confirmed; it is not a first-line screen for hypercortisolism. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Diagnosis of Cushing syndrome)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Diagnosis of Cushing syndrome

options.text: Late-night salivary cortisol on two consecutive nights collected at home with point-of-collection swabs and laboratory cortisol measurement on the samples
options.rationale: Late-night salivary cortisol detects loss of diurnal nadir characteristic of Cushing syndrome and is one of three recommended first-line screening tests with high sensitivity and specificity. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Diagnosis of Cushing syndrome)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Diagnosis of Cushing syndrome

options.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of the pituitary gland with gadolinium contrast performed prior to any biochemical screening to identify a pituitary corticotroph adenoma
options.rationale: Pituitary MRI is reserved for confirmed ACTH-dependent hypercortisolism; pre-biochemistry imaging detects incidentalomas in 10 percent of normal people and misleads workup. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Diagnosis of Cushing syndrome)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Diagnosis of Cushing syndrome

options.text: Insulin tolerance test performed in the endocrine day-stay unit as the first-line dynamic screening method to assess hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis function
options.rationale: Insulin tolerance test assesses ACTH reserve for adrenal insufficiency; it is not used to screen for Cushing syndrome and carries hypoglycaemia risk. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Diagnosis of Cushing syndrome)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Diagnosis of Cushing syndrome

explanation.summary: Cushing syndrome screening uses three recommended first-line tests, any of which can be chosen depending on patient circumstances: late-night salivary cortisol (collected at home, very sensitive for loss of diurnal nadir), 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (cortisol under 50 nmol/L at 08:00 the morning after a 23:00 dose excludes), and 24-hour urinary free cortisol (above three times upper limit of normal is diagnostic). Two abnormal screens are usually required before confirming hypercortisolism. ACTH measurement and dynamic testing (high-dose dexamethasone suppression, CRH stimulation, inferior petrosal sinus sampling) come after confirmed hypercortisolism to localise the source.
key_points: Three first-line screens: salivary cortisol, 1 mg DST, 24-hour UFC
Two abnormal screens needed to confirm hypercortisolism
ACTH differentiates pituitary, ectopic and adrenal sources after confirmation
Pituitary imaging only after ACTH-dependent confirmed
explanation.pearls: Cyclical Cushing syndrome causes intermittent biochemistry; repeat screening is often required over weeks and a single normal late-night salivary cortisol does not exclude when clinical suspicion is high.

sources.label: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Diagnosis of Cushing syndrome

stem: You are the medical registrar in the hypertension clinic reviewing a 38-year-old man with three-drug resistant hypertension and persistent hypokalaemia despite oral potassium replacement. His aldosterone to renin ratio screening test taken in the morning after his usual antihypertensives were optimised to non-interfering agents has returned strongly positive. He has no headaches, palpitations or sweating. CT of the adrenal glands has not yet been arranged and he is keen to know what comes next before any imaging or surgical opinion is sought.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 162/100 on three drugs, HR 76, BMI 28, no orthostatic drop
data_table.Examination: Cardiovascular examination unremarkable; no abdominal bruit; no Cushingoid features; reflexes brisk symmetric
data_table.Investigations: Potassium 3.1 (on supplements 40 mmol daily), sodium 142, creatinine 80; aldosterone:renin ratio 110 (normal under 30); morning aldosterone 720 pmol/L, plasma renin 6.5 mU/L
data_table.Medications: Doxazosin 4 mg daily; verapamil 240 mg daily; hydralazine 50 mg three times daily; potassium chloride 40 mmol daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Proceed directly to CT scan of the adrenal glands today to identify a unilateral adenoma before any confirmatory dynamic biochemical testing has been completed
options.rationale: CT is performed after biochemical confirmation; incidentalomas occur in 4 to 8 percent of adults and CT cannot distinguish functioning aldosterone-producing adenoma from non-functioning incidentaloma. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Primary aldosteronism)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Primary aldosteronism

options.text: Refer immediately for adrenal vein sampling for lateralisation of aldosterone secretion before any dynamic confirmation of autonomous aldosterone production is undertaken
options.rationale: AVS is reserved for confirmed primary aldosteronism in surgical candidates after biochemical confirmation; performing it before confirmation risks unnecessary invasive testing. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Primary aldosteronism)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Primary aldosteronism

options.text: Commence empirical spironolactone 100 mg daily as therapeutic confirmation without further biochemical or radiological investigation in this resistant hypertension setting
options.rationale: Empirical mineralocorticoid receptor antagonism without confirmation misses the opportunity for curative unilateral adrenalectomy in lateralised disease and risks side effects. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Primary aldosteronism)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Primary aldosteronism

options.text: Repeat the aldosterone:renin ratio test in three months on the same medications without confirmation testing and review the trend before any further escalation occurs
options.rationale: A clearly positive ARR with biochemical features warrants confirmatory testing rather than repeat screening; delays diagnosis and risks ongoing cardiovascular damage. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Primary aldosteronism)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Primary aldosteronism

options.text: Perform saline suppression testing under medical supervision in the day-stay unit to confirm autonomous aldosterone secretion before any imaging or surgical consultation
options.rationale: Confirmatory dynamic testing such as saline suppression, oral salt loading, fludrocortisone suppression or captopril challenge is performed after positive ARR before imaging or adrenal vein sampling. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Primary aldosteronism)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Primary aldosteronism

explanation.summary: Primary aldosteronism is the most common secondary cause of hypertension, present in 5 to 10 percent of all hypertensive patients and up to 20 percent of resistant hypertension. Screening uses morning aldosterone to renin ratio after switching to non-interfering antihypertensives (verapamil, hydralazine, doxazosin, prazosin). Positive ARR requires confirmation by one of four dynamic tests (saline suppression, oral salt loading, fludrocortisone suppression, captopril challenge) before subtyping. CT adrenals plus adrenal vein sampling for lateralisation are performed after biochemical confirmation in surgical candidates under 35 with unilateral adenoma on CT or in any patient considering adrenalectomy.
key_points: Confirm ARR with saline or salt loading suppression before imaging
Spironolactone or eplerenone for bilateral or non-surgical disease
Adrenal vein sampling for lateralisation before adrenalectomy
Switch to non-interfering antihypertensives before ARR screen
explanation.pearls: Adrenal vein sampling for primary aldosteronism is operator-dependent and requires cortisol-corrected aldosterone ratios above 4 between sides to confirm lateralisation; centres with low procedural volume should refer.

sources.label: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Primary aldosteronism

stem: You are the medical registrar called to ED to assess a 78-year-old woman on apixaban 5 mg twice daily for atrial fibrillation who has presented with three hours of sudden headache, right hemiparesis and reduced consciousness following a fall at home. CT brain shows a 28 mL left basal ganglia haemorrhage with intraventricular extension. Her last apixaban dose was four hours ago according to her dosette. She has no current anti-fibrinolytic or platelet inhibitor in addition to her DOAC. Her renal function is stable from clinic two weeks ago.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 178/102, HR 78, RR 18, SpO2 96 percent RA, GCS 12 (E3 V4 M5), T 36.6
data_table.Examination: Right hemiparesis 2/5; right facial droop; dysarthria; left pupil 3 mm reactive; chest clear
data_table.Investigations: CT 28 mL left basal ganglia haemorrhage with intraventricular extension; INR 1.1; APTT 38; creatinine 90 (eGFR 56); Hb 124; platelets 220
data_table.Medications: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily; metoprolol 25 mg twice daily; rosuvastatin 20 mg nocte

lead_in: What is the most appropriate reversal agent?

options.text: Intravenous idarucizumab 5 g given as two consecutive 2.5 g boluses as the targeted reversal antibody binding the active anticoagulant directly in the circulation
options.rationale: Idarucizumab reverses dabigatran (direct thrombin inhibitor); it has no effect on factor Xa inhibitors such as apixaban or rivaroxaban. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: Reversal of anticoagulants)
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: Reversal of anticoagulants

options.text: Four-factor prothrombin complex concentrate 25 to 50 units/kg intravenously as the reversal option when andexanet alfa is unavailable in the local hospital
options.rationale: Andexanet alfa is the specific factor Xa inhibitor reversal agent but where unavailable, 4-factor PCC 25 to 50 units/kg is the recommended option for apixaban or rivaroxaban major bleeding. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: Reversal of anticoagulants)
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: Reversal of anticoagulants

options.text: Vitamin K 10 mg intravenously combined with fresh frozen plasma four units transfused over four hours through a peripheral large-bore intravenous access
options.rationale: Vitamin K and FFP reverse warfarin not DOACs; vitamin K has no role in factor Xa inhibitor reversal and FFP volume causes harm without correcting coagulopathy. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: Reversal of anticoagulants)
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: Reversal of anticoagulants

options.text: Protamine sulfate 50 mg intravenously over 10 minutes administered as the recommended reversal agent for any factor Xa inhibitor major bleeding presentation
options.rationale: Protamine reverses unfractionated heparin and partially reverses LMWH; it has no role in DOAC reversal and is not indicated in apixaban bleeding. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: Reversal of anticoagulants)
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: Reversal of anticoagulants

options.text: Activated charcoal 50 g orally via nasogastric tube after intubation as the sole intervention to reduce ongoing absorption of the most recent apixaban dose
options.rationale: Charcoal is reserved for DOAC ingestion within two hours of dose and is adjunctive only; with last dose four hours ago and ICH, primary therapy is haemostatic reversal. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: Reversal of anticoagulants)
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: Reversal of anticoagulants

explanation.summary: Apixaban and rivaroxaban are direct factor Xa inhibitors. In major bleeding (intracranial, retroperitoneal, severe gastrointestinal or any with haemodynamic compromise) the specific reversal agent is andexanet alfa (recombinant decoy factor Xa) given as a bolus and infusion. Where andexanet is unavailable, 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate at 25 to 50 units/kg is the recommended alternative based on observational and limited randomised evidence. Activated charcoal 50 g is given if the last dose was within 2 hours. Tranexamic acid may be added for mucosal bleeding. Dabigatran reversal is specifically idarucizumab. Warfarin is reversed with intravenous vitamin K plus 4-factor PCC.
key_points: Apixaban or rivaroxaban: andexanet alfa, then 4F-PCC if unavailable
Dabigatran: idarucizumab 5 g as two 2.5 g boluses
Warfarin: vitamin K 10 mg IV plus 4F-PCC 25 to 50 units/kg
Charcoal only if DOAC dose within 2 hours
explanation.pearls: Apixaban-specific calibrated anti-Xa activity assay can quantify drug effect when available; standard APTT and INR are insensitive to therapeutic apixaban concentrations.

sources.label: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: Reversal of anticoagulants

stem: You are the GP shared-care registrar reviewing a 26-year-old man with first-episode schizophrenia who started olanzapine 10 mg nocte at discharge from inpatient care 12 weeks ago. His mental state has stabilised and he attends the appointment with his community mental health nurse. He reports increased appetite, has gained 5 kg since discharge and notices a thicker waist. He has no chest pain, no family history of premature cardiovascular disease and is a never smoker. He drinks two standard drinks per week.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78, HR 78, weight 84 kg (was 79 at discharge), waist 102 cm, BMI 27
data_table.Examination: Mental state stable; no extrapyramidal signs; no acanthosis nigricans; cardiovascular examination unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: No bloods since discharge; ECG at discharge QTc 412 ms; HbA1c at discharge 5.4 percent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate investigation panel today?

options.text: Fasting glucose only with deferral of lipid profile until the 12-month annual review according to the standard antipsychotic monitoring schedule recommended for ongoing therapy
options.rationale: Fasting lipids are part of the 12-week panel not deferred to annual review; metabolic effects emerge within weeks and require early detection for intervention. (Source: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatrists: Antipsychotic monitoring)
source_refs: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatrists: Antipsychotic monitoring

options.text: Serum olanzapine concentration as the priority biochemistry test today to assess therapeutic blood drug level guidance for any further olanzapine dose adjustment
options.rationale: Olanzapine therapeutic drug monitoring is reserved for non-response, toxicity or adherence concerns; routine monitoring is metabolic biochemistry rather than serum drug levels. (Source: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatrists: Antipsychotic monitoring)
source_refs: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatrists: Antipsychotic monitoring

options.text: Echocardiogram and 24-hour ambulatory ECG monitoring as the first-line cardiac screening for any patient stable on antipsychotic therapy at the 12-week review milestone
options.rationale: Routine ambulatory cardiac monitoring is not indicated; baseline and 12-week ECG are sufficient unless QTc prolongation, syncope or family sudden death history exists. (Source: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatrists: Antipsychotic monitoring)
source_refs: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatrists: Antipsychotic monitoring

options.text: Fasting lipid profile, fasting glucose or HbA1c, urea and electrolytes, liver function tests and prolactin to complete the 12-week metabolic monitoring panel today
options.rationale: Antipsychotic 12-week metabolic monitoring includes fasting lipids, glucose, weight, blood pressure, waist, prolactin and ECG QTc; this is the time point for the first post-initiation set of bloods. (Source: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatrists: Antipsychotic monitoring)
source_refs: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatrists: Antipsychotic monitoring

options.text: Sleep-deprived electroencephalogram and brain magnetic resonance imaging as the standard structural and electrophysiological review at three months of antipsychotic therapy
options.rationale: Routine EEG and MRI are not part of antipsychotic monitoring; they are reserved for atypical presentations, new neurological signs or specific clinical suspicion. (Source: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatrists: Antipsychotic monitoring)
source_refs: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatrists: Antipsychotic monitoring

explanation.summary: Antipsychotic metabolic monitoring follows a structured schedule: baseline (weight, waist, BP, FBC, UEC, LFTs, fasting lipids, fasting glucose or HbA1c, prolactin, ECG), then weight and BP weekly for the first 4 weeks, weight and BP at 12 weeks, then fasting biochemistry at 12 weeks, 6 months and annually. Olanzapine and clozapine have the highest metabolic risk; aripiprazole and lurasidone the lowest. Intervention triggers include weight gain over 5 percent, waist increase over 5 cm, new dyslipidaemia, prediabetes (HbA1c 5.7 to 6.4 percent) or diabetes (HbA1c above 6.5 percent), and warrant lifestyle counselling and consideration of switching to a lower-risk agent.
key_points: Baseline plus 12 weeks plus 6 months plus annual biochemistry
Olanzapine and clozapine highest metabolic risk
Switch trigger: weight above 5 percent gain or new dyslipidaemia or dysglycaemia
Prolactin only if clinical symptoms of hyperprolactinaemia
explanation.pearls: Adding metformin 500 mg twice daily reduces antipsychotic-induced weight gain and improves insulin sensitivity in patients with significant weight gain who must remain on the offending agent.

sources.label: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatrists: Antipsychotic monitoring

stem: You are the GP reviewing a 42-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder who has been stable on lithium carbonate 1000 mg nocte for the past five years with euthymia and full functional recovery. She attends every six months for routine review. She has no symptoms of lithium toxicity, no tremor and no polyuria. She uses ibuprofen occasionally for menstrual pain but has not taken any over the past two weeks. She has no acute illness or change in fluid status today and is keen to complete her routine 12-monthly biochemistry panel.

data_table.Examination: No fine tremor; no goitre; cognition intact; cardiovascular and neurological examination unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: Last six months: lithium 0.78 mmol/L, creatinine 75, TSH 2.1, calcium 2.42; today's bloods not yet drawn
data_table.Medications: Lithium carbonate 1000 mg nocte; quetiapine 50 mg nocte for sleep; combined contraceptive pill

lead_in: What is the most appropriate investigation panel today?

options.text: Lithium concentration, urea, electrolytes and creatinine, estimated glomerular filtration rate, thyroid function, calcium and weight as the comprehensive annual monitoring
options.rationale: Lithium 12-month review covers lithium concentration plus renal function, thyroid function and calcium to detect nephropathy, hypothyroidism and hyperparathyroidism as long-term adverse effects. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic

options.text: Lithium concentration alone given that recent thyroid and renal function were normal at six months and she remains clinically euthymic without tremor or polyuria
options.rationale: Annual review includes lithium, U and E, eGFR, TSH and calcium together; lithium concentration alone misses the cumulative organ-specific toxicities that emerge over time. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic

options.text: Lithium concentration plus full blood count and liver function tests as the annual review panel reflecting standard antipsychotic monitoring transposed onto her current bipolar therapy
options.rationale: FBC and LFTs are not specifically affected by lithium; renal function, thyroid function and calcium are the lithium-specific surveillance tests required annually. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic

options.text: Echocardiogram and 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure as a cardiovascular screen reflecting concern about long-term lithium-related cardiovascular toxicity in stable euthymic patients
options.rationale: Routine echocardiogram is not part of lithium monitoring; ECG at baseline is recommended but ongoing cardiac imaging is reserved for symptoms or specific risk factors. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic

options.text: Defer all blood tests until next clinically indicated review or annual review the following year because she has been entirely stable on her current dose for the past five years
options.rationale: Annual review is mandatory regardless of clinical stability because lithium nephropathy and hypothyroidism develop insidiously over years; deferring misses early intervention. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic

explanation.summary: Lithium long-term monitoring includes lithium concentration (target 0.4 to 0.8 mmol/L for maintenance, 0.6 to 1.0 mmol/L for acute mania, taken 12 hours post-dose), renal function (U, E, creatinine, eGFR), thyroid function (TSH, free T4 if abnormal) and calcium (lithium causes hyperparathyroidism in around 5 to 10 percent of long-term users). Frequency is 3-monthly for the first 6 months then 6-monthly to 12-monthly for stable patients. ECG at baseline. Toxicity risk increases with NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, thiazides, dehydration and reduced renal function; review medications and fluid status at every visit.
key_points: Annual lithium, UEC, eGFR, TSH, calcium for stable patients
Maintenance target 0.4 to 0.8 mmol/L 12 hours post-dose
NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, thiazides raise lithium concentration
Hyperparathyroidism prevalence 5 to 10 percent in long-term lithium
explanation.pearls: An eGFR fall over 25 percent within a year on lithium warrants nephrology review and consideration of switching to valproate or lamotrigine; nephropathy may progress despite lithium cessation.

sources.label: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatrists: Bipolar guidelines

stem:
You are the GP reviewing a 48-year-old man who wants to stop smoking. He has smoked 20 cigarettes per day for 30 years and his Heaviness of Smoking Index is high (first cigarette within 5 minutes of waking). He has never tried pharmacotherapy. He has well-controlled depression on sertraline 100 mg daily for the past three years with no recent suicidal ideation.

He has no cardiovascular disease and no current alcohol use. He is keen to quit before his daughter's wedding in six months and wants the most effective pharmacotherapy.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 130/82, HR 76, BMI 26, expired carbon monoxide 18 ppm
data_table.Examination: No respiratory distress; chest clear; mental state euthymic with no suicidality elicited on screen
data_table.Investigations: Lipid profile shows mildly elevated LDL; baseline ECG sinus rhythm, QTc 410 ms

lead_in: What is the most effective first-line pharmacotherapy option?

options.text: Nicotine replacement therapy patch 21 mg over 24 hours combined with as-needed nicotine lozenge 4 mg for breakthrough craving across the standard quit period
options.rationale: Combination NRT is effective first-line but varenicline has higher quit rates at 12 weeks in heavy smokers; both are valid choices, varenicline preferred where suitable. (Source: RACGP: Supporting smoking cessation)
source_refs: RACGP: Supporting smoking cessation

options.text: Bupropion sustained release 150 mg daily for three days then 150 mg twice daily for 12 weeks, started two weeks before set quit date with seizure precautions discussed
options.rationale: Bupropion is effective but has lower quit rates than varenicline and a seizure risk; it is reserved when varenicline and NRT are contraindicated or have failed. (Source: RACGP: Supporting smoking cessation)
source_refs: RACGP: Supporting smoking cessation

options.text: Varenicline 0.5 mg daily for three days, then 0.5 mg twice daily for four days, then 1 mg twice daily for 12 weeks with set quit date one week into therapy
options.rationale: Varenicline has the highest quit rates at 12 weeks for heavy smokers and is first-line where not contraindicated; titration over week 1 reduces nausea before reaching maintenance dose. (Source: RACGP: Supporting smoking cessation)
source_refs: RACGP: Supporting smoking cessation

options.text: Behavioural counselling alone via the Quitline telephone service with no pharmacotherapy because his depression contraindicates all pharmacological options for cessation today
options.rationale: Stable depression on SSRI is not a contraindication to varenicline; combining behavioural support with pharmacotherapy is more effective than counselling alone. (Source: RACGP: Supporting smoking cessation)
source_refs: RACGP: Supporting smoking cessation

options.text: Electronic nicotine delivery system (vape) prescription as the first-line cessation therapy with the goal of complete vape cessation by three months after quit date achieved
options.rationale: Nicotine vaping is reserved for patients who have failed first-line pharmacotherapy combined with behavioural support; it is not first-line under TGA prescription pathway. (Source: RACGP: Supporting smoking cessation)
source_refs: RACGP: Supporting smoking cessation

explanation.summary: Smoking cessation pharmacotherapy options ranked by quit rate are varenicline (most effective), combination nicotine replacement therapy (patch plus oral form for breakthrough cravings), single-form NRT, bupropion sustained release and nortriptyline. Varenicline is started one week before quit date, titrated over the first week and continued for 12 weeks; an additional 12 weeks reduces relapse. Stable depression is not a contraindication to varenicline but patients should be monitored for neuropsychiatric changes. Cardiovascular events are uncommon but warrant caution in recent acute coronary syndrome. Behavioural support (Quitline, brief intervention) doubles quit rates whatever pharmacotherapy is chosen.
key_points: Varenicline first-line for heavy smokers without contraindication
Combination NRT (patch plus oral) is second-line
Start one week before set quit date; titrate to reduce nausea
Behavioural support doubles quit rates when added to pharmacotherapy
explanation.pearls: Varenicline can be safely continued in cardiovascular disease except recent acute coronary syndrome (within 2 weeks) where waiting for stabilisation is reasonable; benefits of quitting outweigh risks.

stem:
You are the GP reviewing a 28-year-old woman who self-refers requesting assessment for adult attention deficit hyperactivity disorder. She describes lifelong difficulty sustaining attention, procrastinating on tasks, losing her keys regularly and missing deadlines at her marketing job. She recalls being told she daydreamed in primary school but never received formal assessment. She is otherwise well, with no past psychiatric history.

She does not use illicit substances. She has read about adult ADHD online and asks what the next step in formal diagnosis would be from primary care today.

data_table.Examination: Mental state: euthymic; thought form linear; no psychotic features; cognition grossly intact
data_table.Investigations: Recent FBC, UEC, TFT, B12 normal; ECG sinus rhythm with QTc 405 ms; not pregnant

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Prescribe a trial of methylphenidate immediate release 10 mg twice daily for two weeks as a diagnostic trial to confirm adult ADHD by symptomatic response
options.rationale: Stimulant prescription is restricted to authorised paediatric, psychiatric or other authorised prescribers in Australia and cannot be initiated empirically without specialist diagnosis. (Source: RANZCP: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: RANZCP: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD

options.text: Order overnight polysomnography and brain magnetic resonance imaging as the standard initial workup investigations for any presentation of adult attentional concerns
options.rationale: Routine imaging and polysomnography are not required for adult ADHD diagnosis; they are reserved for atypical presentations such as new-onset symptoms or focal neurology. (Source: RANZCP: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: RANZCP: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily empirically as initial pharmacotherapy because anxiety is more likely than ADHD in adult women presenting with attentional symptoms
options.rationale: Empirical antidepressant treatment without diagnosis risks missing primary ADHD and delays correct treatment; specialist diagnostic assessment should occur first. (Source: RANZCP: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: RANZCP: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD

options.text: Reassure her that adult-onset ADHD is not a recognised diagnostic entity and recommend cognitive behavioural therapy for non-specific attention concerns from primary care
options.rationale: Adult ADHD is a recognised diagnosis when childhood symptoms can be demonstrated to predate age 12; dismissing the presentation denies access to evidence-based treatment. (Source: RANZCP: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: RANZCP: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD

options.text: Refer to a psychiatrist or paediatrician authorised to assess and prescribe for adult ADHD with collateral history from childhood and adult symptom rating scales
options.rationale: Adult ADHD diagnosis requires specialist assessment including DSM-5 criteria, childhood symptom evidence (school reports, parental collateral), adult symptom scales and exclusion of differentials. (Source: RANZCP: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: RANZCP: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD diagnosis requires DSM-5 criteria: five or more inattention or hyperactivity-impulsivity symptoms (over six for under 17), symptoms present in two or more settings, evidence symptoms began before age 12, and significant functional impairment with other causes excluded. Workup includes childhood collateral (school reports, parents), adult self-report scales (Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale, Connors), psychiatric history, substance use review and basic biochemistry to exclude differentials. Stimulant prescription in Australia is restricted to authorised psychiatrists, paediatricians and selected other specialists under state-based authority schedules. GP shared-care arrangements are common after stabilisation.
key_points: DSM-5 requires childhood onset before age 12
Specialist diagnostic assessment in Australia before stimulant prescription
Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale is a validated screening tool
Comorbidity with anxiety, depression and substance use is common
explanation.pearls: Stimulant prescribing in Australia requires individual state-based authority approval which is renewed annually; GPs may continue prescribing under shared care after specialist initiation in most jurisdictions.

sources.label: RANZCP: Australian evidence-based clinical practice guideline for ADHD

stem: You are the GP reviewing a 35-year-old woman who was the driver in a serious motor vehicle accident eight months ago. She describes intrusive flashbacks, hypervigilance, avoiding driving, nightmares disrupting sleep four nights a week, and irritability with her partner. Symptoms have not improved spontaneously and now meet DSM-5 criteria for post-traumatic stress disorder. She has no history of depression, no current suicidal ideation, no substance misuse and is keen to engage in evidence-based therapy. She is wary of medication and asks what will help her most.

data_table.Examination: Mental state: anxious affect, no psychotic features; cognition intact; insight retained
data_table.Investigations: Recent FBC, UEC, TFT all normal; PTSD Checklist score 56 of 80

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily titrated to 200 mg as the first-line pharmacological treatment in preference to trauma-focused psychological therapies for this presentation
options.rationale: SSRIs are effective but trauma-focused psychological therapies have superior evidence as first-line; pharmacotherapy is reserved for inadequate response or patient preference. (Source: Phoenix Australia: Australian guidelines for the prevention and treatment of PTSD)
source_refs: Phoenix Australia: Australian guidelines for the prevention and treatment of PTSD

options.text: Refer for trauma-focused cognitive behavioural therapy or eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing therapy as the evidence-based first-line treatment in PTSD
options.rationale: Trauma-focused CBT and EMDR are the first-line psychological treatments for PTSD with the strongest evidence base for symptom reduction and sustained remission. (Source: Phoenix Australia: Australian guidelines for the prevention and treatment of PTSD)
source_refs: Phoenix Australia: Australian guidelines for the prevention and treatment of PTSD

options.text: Prescribe prazosin 1 mg nocte and a long-acting benzodiazepine for sleep restoration and to suppress traumatic re-experiencing symptoms before any psychological therapy starts
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not recommended in PTSD as they impair extinction learning and worsen long-term outcomes; prazosin alone is reserved for nightmare reduction adjunctively. (Source: Phoenix Australia: Australian guidelines for the prevention and treatment of PTSD)
source_refs: Phoenix Australia: Australian guidelines for the prevention and treatment of PTSD

options.text: Provide supportive counselling alone through generalist primary care without referral to specific evidence-based trauma-focused psychological therapy in the first six months
options.rationale: Generic supportive counselling is less effective than trauma-focused therapy and does not address core PTSD symptoms; first-line trauma-focused therapy should be offered. (Source: Phoenix Australia: Australian guidelines for the prevention and treatment of PTSD)
source_refs: Phoenix Australia: Australian guidelines for the prevention and treatment of PTSD

options.text: Refer for inpatient psychiatric admission to a tertiary trauma service for intensive group-based therapy because symptoms have persisted for eight months without intervention
options.rationale: Inpatient admission is reserved for risk to self or others, severe functional decompensation or treatment failure; outpatient trauma-focused therapy is first-line. (Source: Phoenix Australia: Australian guidelines for the prevention and treatment of PTSD)
source_refs: Phoenix Australia: Australian guidelines for the prevention and treatment of PTSD

explanation.summary: PTSD first-line treatment is trauma-focused psychological therapy: trauma-focused cognitive behavioural therapy (including prolonged exposure and cognitive processing therapy) or eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing. Both have strong evidence for symptom reduction over 8 to 12 weekly sessions. Pharmacotherapy (SSRI sertraline, paroxetine or fluoxetine) is reserved for patients who decline or cannot access psychological therapy, who have inadequate response, or who have comorbid depression. Benzodiazepines are contraindicated as they impair extinction learning and worsen long-term outcomes. Prazosin is an evidence-based adjunctive treatment for distressing nightmares.
key_points: First-line: TF-CBT or EMDR for 8 to 12 sessions
Sertraline, paroxetine, fluoxetine: SSRIs as second-line
Benzodiazepines contraindicated in PTSD
Prazosin adjunctive for distressing nightmares
explanation.pearls: Prazosin 1 mg nocte titrated weekly to 5 to 10 mg specifically reduces PTSD trauma nightmares; first-dose hypotension warrants supine titration and warning patients about postural symptoms.

sources.label: Phoenix Australia: Australian guidelines for the prevention and treatment of PTSD

stem: You are the GP reviewing a 26-year-old woman with established borderline personality disorder confirmed by community mental health team assessment two years ago. She has a five-year history of recurrent superficial wrist cutting in response to perceived rejection, two prior low-lethality paracetamol overdoses in the past three years, and unstable interpersonal relationships. She has previously had supportive counselling without sustained benefit. She is not currently suicidal and is engaged in care. She asks what intervention would most reduce her self-harm and admissions over time.

data_table.Examination: Old linear scars on bilateral forearms; mental state: euthymic, no acute risk; cognition intact
data_table.Investigations: Recent FBC, UEC, LFTs, urine drug screen all normal; no current pregnancy
data_table.Medications: Quetiapine 50 mg nocte for sleep; nil other

lead_in: What is the most appropriate evidence-based recommendation?

options.text: Commence high-dose fluoxetine 60 mg daily as first-line pharmacotherapy for the core features of borderline personality disorder with quarterly review and dose titration
options.rationale: No medication has approval or strong evidence as first-line treatment for borderline personality disorder; structured psychological therapy is the cornerstone of management. (Source: NHMRC: Clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder)
source_refs: NHMRC: Clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder

options.text: Refer for inpatient psychiatric admission whenever she presents with self-harm to interrupt the behaviour and provide containment until stable for outpatient discharge
options.rationale: Routine admission for self-harm in borderline personality disorder reinforces the behaviour and worsens long-term outcomes; brief crisis admissions are used only when imminent risk warrants. (Source: NHMRC: Clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder)
source_refs: NHMRC: Clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder

options.text: Recommend electroconvulsive therapy as the next escalation in management given previous failure of supportive counselling and ongoing high-risk behaviours over years
options.rationale: ECT is reserved for severe treatment-resistant depression or catatonia; it has no role in core borderline personality disorder treatment without comorbid mood diagnosis. (Source: NHMRC: Clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder)
source_refs: NHMRC: Clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder

options.text: Refer for a structured dialectical behaviour therapy program combining weekly individual therapy with group skills training over 12 months as the recommended intervention
options.rationale: Dialectical behaviour therapy has the strongest evidence base for reducing self-harm, hospital admissions and treatment dropout in borderline personality disorder over a 12-month program. (Source: NHMRC: Clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder)
source_refs: NHMRC: Clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder

options.text: Continue supportive counselling alone in primary care without escalation to specialist evidence-based psychotherapy because she has stabilised on her current management plan
options.rationale: Generic supportive counselling has not reduced her self-harm or admissions; evidence-based structured therapy such as DBT, mentalisation-based therapy or schema therapy is indicated. (Source: NHMRC: Clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder)
source_refs: NHMRC: Clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder

explanation.summary: Borderline personality disorder is best treated with structured evidence-based psychological therapy. Dialectical behaviour therapy (12 months of weekly individual therapy plus group skills training) has the strongest evidence and is first-line where available. Other evidence-based therapies include mentalisation-based therapy, schema-focused therapy and transference-focused psychotherapy. No medication is licensed for borderline personality disorder; pharmacotherapy targets comorbid depression, anxiety, ADHD or PTSD when present. Crisis admissions are used briefly for imminent suicide risk only; planned brief admissions during crises help avoid prolonged admissions that risk worsening dependence and identity confusion.
key_points: DBT first-line evidence-based therapy for BPD
Mentalisation-based therapy and schema therapy are alternatives
No first-line pharmacotherapy approved for core symptoms
Brief crisis admissions only; avoid prolonged inpatient stays
explanation.pearls: Dialectical behaviour therapy includes mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation and interpersonal effectiveness skills modules; commitment to the full 12-month structure is the strongest predictor of outcome.

sources.label: NHMRC: Clinical practice guideline for the management of borderline personality disorder
